Event: Tom bullies Greg on first meeting, asking if he'd kiss him
Explanation: Asserting dominance mildly boosts Tom’s ego while stressing and humiliating Greg.

Event: Tom reacts with jealousy when Greg moves in with Kendall
Explanation: His jealousy causes him distress without affecting Greg’s material position.

Event: Tom compares his and Greg’s relationship to Nero and Sporus and uses it to bind Greg closer
Explanation: He deepens Greg’s emotional/psychological entanglement to secure loyalty at Greg’s expense.

Event: Tom kisses Greg’s forehead to celebrate not going to prison
Explanation: The gesture comforts Greg and expresses Tom’s joy, benefiting both relationally.

Event: Tom is unhappy and jealous when Greg wants to ask out Comfrey
Explanation: His jealousy upsets him and applies possessive pressure on Greg with little upside.

Event: Tom invites Greg to join Logan against the siblings, framed as a “marriage proposal”
Explanation: He secures a political ally and patronage for himself while pulling Greg into a morally compromised side.

Event: Tom advises Greg not to trust anyone in business
Explanation: The advice realistically helps Greg navigate a predatory environment and costs Tom little.

Event: Tom withholds Greg’s confidential meeting with Michelle Pantsil from others
Explanation: He protects Greg’s secret while gaining private leverage, with limited harm shown.

Event: Tom uses an employee as a physical footstool
Explanation: He indulges his dominance with no real benefit to others and clear humiliation for the employee.

Event: Tom encourages a hesitant Greg to join him as footstool
Explanation: He pressures Greg into degrading complicity, tightening control more than offering benefit.

Event: Tom praises Greg for blackmailing him and offers more than demanded
Explanation: He reframes blackmail into a negotiation, giving Greg more upside and slightly stabilizing their power dynamic.

Event: Tom openly admits he loves being rich and refuses to deny himself luxuries
Explanation: He gains personal clarity and comfort while signaling selfishness and alienating others.

Event: Tom suddenly obsesses over Shiv’s periods due to fear of prison
Explanation: His anxiety-driven baby trap harms Shiv emotionally and doesn’t materially solve his prison fear.

Event: Tom gets deeply involved in wedding details and is horrible to the wedding planner
Explanation: He satisfies his control impulses at the planner’s expense.

Event: Tom delivers brutally honest attacks on Shiv’s attitude and fitness for motherhood
Explanation: The venting damages Shiv and their relationship without yielding him any clear benefit.

Event: Tom throws water bottles at Greg when Greg wants to “see other people” professionally
Explanation: His tantrum physically and emotionally intimidates Greg and makes Tom look unstable.

Event: Tom pushes a woman aside during a shooting scare, saying “executives are coming through”
Explanation: He marginally increases his own safety while directly endangering someone else.

Event: Tom humiliates Shiv’s lover at the wedding and has him removed
Explanation: He asserts boundaries and emotional satisfaction while socially humiliating Nate.

Event: Tom tells Shiv to “fuck off” when she mocks him too far at dinner
Explanation: He finally asserts a boundary, benefiting his self-respect and giving Shiv needed feedback.

Event: Tom tells Shiv he’s considering leaving her
Explanation: The threat signals limits, potentially prompting change and clarifying his own position.

Event: Tom betrays Shiv and the siblings by warning Logan of their plan to block the sale
Explanation: He secures power and proximity to Logan while gravely harming the siblings’ leverage.

Event: Tom sides with Matsson in the GoJo deal and takes over as Waystar CEO
Explanation: He gains top office and status while enabling a sale that strips power from the Roy kids and likely harms staff.

Event: Tom uses human furniture and relishes the idea of firing fifty ATN people
Explanation: He enjoys and consolidates power by inflicting job loss and humiliation on subordinates.

Event: Tom willingly offers himself as the fall guy to go to prison for Waystar
Explanation: He incurs extreme personal risk partly to protect the company and others, hoping vaguely for future reward.

Event: Tom misses Logan’s funeral to work at Waystar
Explanation: He boosts his standing with Matsson while slighting familial/relational obligations.

Event: Tom overturns Greg’s desk in joy after learning he’s not going to prison
Explanation: He celebrates at Greg’s expense, causing disruption without Greg’s consent.

Event: Tom checks on Kendall’s son after Logan harms him at Thanksgiving
Explanation: He shows rare concern, giving comfort at negligible cost to himself.

Event: Tom chooses to learn about Waystar’s illegal activities instead of keeping plausible deniability
Explanation: He increases his personal legal exposure while positioning himself to potentially manage or expose wrongdoing.

Event: Tom involves Greg in knowledge of the illegal cruise activities
Explanation: He offloads risk and labor onto Greg, gaining shared culpability leverage over him.

Event: Tom tells Greg to trust no one and warns that even he can’t be fully trusted
Explanation: His candor realistically helps Greg assess him and the environment, albeit cynically.

Event: Tom keeps Greg’s meeting with Michelle Pantsil secret
Explanation: He protects Greg’s risk-taking while preserving a bond and leverage for himself.

Event: Tom sucks up to Logan from his first scene and throughout
Explanation: He accrues patronage and protection through sycophancy that enables Logan’s power.

Event: Tom continues to be submissive to Matsson, even as Matsson insults him and lusts after Shiv
Explanation: He tolerates personal humiliation to secure the CEO role and Matsson’s favor.

Event: Tom accepts being a puppet CEO under Matsson
Explanation: He gains title and material rewards while ceding real authority and participating in Matsson’s agenda.

Event: Tom protects Greg from Logan’s wrath after Greg betrays Ewan to a biographer
Explanation: He risks his own standing with Logan to shield Greg with no clear compensating gain.

Event: Tom agrees to absorb Greg’s personal blame in the cruise scandal with no quid pro quo
Explanation: He takes on serious potential liability solely to spare Greg.

Event: Tom decides to back Gil Eavis out of idealism
Explanation: His idealistic support offers him limited personal benefit while aiming at broader systemic good.

Event: Tom mocks employees’ distress as Greg fires them over video call
Explanation: He extracts amusement and a sense of power from their misery without aiding anyone.

Event: Tom takes advantage of Logan calling him “son” to ingratiate himself further
Explanation: He opportunistically leverages Logan’s confusion to deepen his own position.

Event: Tom grudgingly accepts Shiv’s offer of an open marriage while not wanting details
Explanation: He suppresses his discomfort to keep the relationship, sacrificing his emotional well-being.

Event: Tom yells at Greg to “man up” and drop his principles about ATN
Explanation: He pressures Greg to conform to immoral work norms, making Greg easier to use.

Event: Tom considers taking Shiv’s last name to aid his social climbing
Explanation: He uses the symbolism of marriage mainly to boost his own status.

Event: Tom pelts Greg with water bottles when Greg tries to break up professionally
Explanation: He vents anger in a way that harms Greg and further undermines his own image and relationship.

Event: Tom becomes increasingly cruel to Shiv, treating her like property while she’s pregnant
Explanation: His escalating cruelty damages Shiv and their bond without giving him durable advantage.

Event: Tom tells Shiv she’s incapable of love and unfit for motherhood while she’s pregnant
Explanation: The attack is deeply wounding and destabilizing, and only worsens his emotional and strategic position.

Event: Tom helps scrub evidence of the cruise scandal once reform is blocked
Explanation: He protects his career and the company by perpetuating harm and denying justice.

Event: Tom humiliates Shiv’s lover at the wedding reception with a “who’s laughing now” moment
Explanation: He gains emotional vindication and status while socially crushing Nate.

Event: Tom performs a Wounded Gazelle Gambit, offering himself as the fall guy who “won’t wriggle”
Explanation: He tries to buy protection with self-sacrifice, exposing himself to major risk for uncertain favor.

Event: Tom assumes Caroline’s cruelty to Shiv is just a joke and laughs along
Explanation: His misreading reinforces Shiv’s abuse dynamics and costs him genuine understanding without helping him much.

Event: Tom continues to act as a Yes-Man to Logan despite seeing how badly Logan treats Shiv
Explanation: He prioritizes personal advancement by echoing Logan while further isolating Shiv.
